elements.sent-2411-001: Strauss$^{1 , $ \footnote{Author to whom correspondence should be addressed: hank@hrsfusion.com {$^1$ HRS Fusion, West Orange, NJ 07052 \end{center.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Resistive wall tearing modes (RWTM) can cause major disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-003: A signature of RWTMs is that the rational surface is sufficiently close to the wall.
elements.sent-2411-004: For $(m,n) = (2,1)$ modes, at normalized minor radius $\rho = 0.75$, the value of $q$ is $q_{75 < 2.$ This is confirmed in simulations and theory and in a DIII-D locked mode disruption database.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The $q_{75 < 2$ criterion is valid at high $\beta$ as well as at low $\beta.$ A very important feature of RWTMs is that they produce major disruptions only when the $q_{75 < 2$ criterion is satisfied.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: If it is not satisfied, or if the wall is ideally conducting, then the mode does not produce a major disruption, although it can produce a minor disruption.
elements.sent-2411-007: Feedback, or rotation of the mode at the wall by complex feedback, can emulate an ideal wall, preventing major disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The $q_{75}$ criterion is analyzed in a linear simulations, and a simple geometric model is given.
elements.sent-2411-009: Resistive wall tearing modes (RWTM) can cause cause major disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-010: This is based on evidence from theory, simulations, and experimental data \cite{jet21,iter21,d3d22,mst23,model,nf-iaea24 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, DIII-D locked mode shot 154576 \cite{sweeney experienced a major disruption.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Linear simulations \cite{d3d22 found the reconstructed equilibrium was stable with an ideal wall.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Nonlinear simulations found a complete thermal quench, and agreement with the experimental thermal quench (TQ) time and the amplitude of the perturbed magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-014: For $(m,n) = (2,1)$ modes, the rational surface radius of the $q=2$ surface, normalized to the plasma minor radius, is $\rho_{q2 > 0.75.$ This can also be expressed as the value of $q$ at $\rho = 0.75$, $q_{75 < 2.$ This is confirmed in simulations and theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Experimentally, it is the disruption criterion in a DIII-D locked mode disruption database.
elements.sent-2411-016: The importance of mode locking and disruption precursors is discussed.
elements.sent-2411-017: The $q_{75 < 2$ criterion is valid at high $\beta$ as well as at low $\beta.$ This is verified experimentally as well as in simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-018: A very important feature of RWTMs is that they produce major disruptions when the $q_{75 < 2$ or $\rho_{q2 > 0.75$ criterion is satisfied.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: If the wall is ideally conducting, then the mode becomes a tearing mode and does not produce a major disruption, although it can produce a minor disruption.
elements.sent-2411-020: Feedback, or rotation of the mode at the wall by complex feedback, can emulate an ideal wall.
elements.sent-2411-021: This implies that RWTMs can be made to act like tearing modes with an ideal wall, and only produce minor disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-022: This is verified experimentally and in simulations at low and high $\beta.$ The $q_{75}$ criterion for a RWTM implies that the $q = 2$ rational surface is sufficiently close to the wall to interact with it.
elements.sent-2411-023: This is analyzed in a linear model, and a simple geometric model is given of the wall interaction criterion.
elements.sent-2411-024: The criterion depends weakly on the ratio of $\rho_{q2 / \rho_w,$ where $\rho_w$ is the wall radius normalized to plasma radius.}
elements.sent-2411-025: For $\rho_w > 1.5,$ the wall is too far away for a RWTM and feedback stabilization is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-026: The criterion is also obtained for general $(m,n),$ and requires the rational surface to be closer to the wall.
elements.sent-2411-027: The outline of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-028: The domain of instability of RWTMs in the $(q_{75 ,\beta)$ plane is presented qualitatively in \rsec{param .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Also shown is the $\rho_{q2 > 0.75$ database of DIII-D locked mode disruptions.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The relevance of mode locking, precursors, edge cooling, and current contraction are discussed.
elements.sent-2411-031: In \rsec{lowbeta , simulations are presented of a sequence of equilibria in which major disruptions occur with a resistive wall, when $q_{75 < 2,$ otherwise a minor disruption occurs.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: If the wall is ideal, only a minor disruption occurs.
elements.sent-2411-033: In a particular example, it is demonstrated that feedback or wall rotation give a similar result as an ideal wall.
elements.sent-2411-034: The amplitude of the non axisymmetric $n > 1$ perturbations is much larger when the wall is resistive, in comparison with an ideal wall, feedback or rotating wall.
elements.sent-2411-035: The computational model used for feedback and rotating wall is discussed in \rsec{feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Finite $\beta$ experimental results in NSXT are presented in \rsec{nstx .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The experiment shows a feedback limited tearing mode, with $\rho_{q2 \approx 0.75,$ evidently close enough to the wall to be feedback controlled.}
elements.sent-2411-038: For a resistive wall, a major disruption occurs.
elements.sent-2411-039: With an ideal wall, feedback, or rotating wall, only minor disruptions occur.
elements.sent-2411-040: The amplitude of the $n > 1$ perturbations is much larger for a resistive wall than for ideal wall, feedback, or rotating wall, as in \rsec{lowbeta .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The reason for the $q_{75}$ criterion is analyzed in \rsec{q75 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The critical value of $\rho_{q2 / \rho_w$ is obtained from linear stability of model equilibria.}
elements.sent-2411-043: It is in good agreement with a simple geometric model.
elements.sent-2411-044: The $\rho_{q2 > 0.75$ criterion occurs for $\rho_w = 1.2,$ as in DIII-D, the model equilibria of \rsec{lowbeta , and NSTX.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The critical $\rho_{q2}$ depends weakly on $\rho_w,$ for $\rho_w \le 1.5.$ A summary is provided in \rsec{summary .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The expression $\rho_{q2 = 0.75,$ can be written as $q_{75 = 2,$ where $q_{75 = q(\rho_{q2 = .75),$ which is useful to represent the RWTM unstable parameter space.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The RWTM is unstable for $q_{75 \le 2.$ The RWM beta limit is approximately the Troyon \cite{troyon no wall limit $\beta_{N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The RWTM is unstable below the RWM limit \cite{betti,villamora .}
elements.sent-2411-049: The labeled points correspond to low and high $\beta$ examples in \rsec{lowbeta , \rsec{nstx , and \rsec{nstxsim .}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Both low and high $\beta$ RWTM and RWM can be limited to minor disruptions by feedback, rotation, or an ideal wall.
elements.sent-2411-051: Locking with resistive wall and without feedback or rotation allows a major disruption.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=4.5cm]{\figdir/fig1a_mst_rwtm4b.eps}(a) \includegraphics[height=4.5cm]{\figdir/fig1b_sweeney2017-fig21.eps}(b) \end{center} \caption{\it (a) Schematic diagram of RWM and RWTM stability in $(q_{75},\beta)$ space. (b) Disruptivity in a DIII-D locked mode disruption database. Reproduced from \cite{sweeney2017}} \label{fig:q75beta}
elements.sent-2411-052: Mode locking means that toroidal rotation stops, destabilizing tearing modes \cite{sweeney,gerasimov2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Sheared rotation stabilizes tearing modes \cite{coelho,wang,drift , including RWTMs \cite{finn95 .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This could be explained by edge cooling, which produces current contraction \cite{schuller,wesson , and can cause $\rho_{q2}$ to increase.}
elements.sent-2411-055: A current contraction model \cite{model is discussed in \rsec{q75 .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Current contraction is caused by edge cooling, which in turn can have several causes.
elements.sent-2411-057: One possible cause is overlapping tearing modes in the edge region, called a $T_{e,q2}$ collapse \cite{sweeney .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Another possibility is resistive ballooning turbulence, proposed as an explanation of the Greenwald density limit \cite{ricci .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Another possible cause of edge cooling is impurity radiation \cite{pucella .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The impurity content might be raised by increasing the plasma density to the Greenwald limit \cite{gates .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The impurities might be introduced purposefully, as in massive gas injection \cite{izzo , or accidentally, as UFOs, pieces of plasma facing tiles falling into the plasma.}
elements.sent-2411-062: All these have been called causes of disruptions, but they are really precursors, cooling the edge and destabilizing a RWTM.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=3.8cm]{\figdir/fig2a_mst_3665841e2q75.qproLb.eps}(a) \includegraphics[height=3.8cm]{\figdir/fig2b_mst_3752129fgL2.eps}(b) \end{center} \caption{\it (a) $q$ profiles of model equilibria as a function of major radius $x = R - R_0$ for model equilibria with $q_a = 2, 2.3, 3, 3.4.$ All but $q_a = 3.4$ have $q_{75} < 2.$ (b) time histories of case $q_a = 3$ with ideal, resistive, feedback, and rotating wall boundary conditions. In all but the resistive wall case, only a minor disruption occurs. } \label{fig:qmst}
elements.sent-2411-063: Simulations were performed with M3D \cite{m3d for a sequence of modified MST equilibria \cite{model,nf-iaea24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Here the results are extended by including simulations with feedback and wall rotation, as discussed in \rsec{feedback .}
elements.sent-2411-065: The simulations had parameters: Lundquist number $S = 10^5,$ wall Lundquist number $S_w = \tau_w / \tau_A = 10^3,$ where $\tau_w$ is the wall penetration time and $\tau_A$ is the \Alf time, and parallel thermal conductivity $\kappa_\parallel = 10 R^2 / \tau_A.$ The simulation had $16$ toroidal planes.
elements.sent-2411-066: The case $q_a = 3.4$ is RWTM marginally stable.
elements.sent-2411-067: Nonlinear simulations with M3D \cite{m3d were initialized with these equilibria.}
elements.sent-2411-068: It was shown \cite{model that with an ideal wall, all the equilibria are unstable only to minor disruptions.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For $q_a \le 3,$ $q_{75 < 2,$ with a resistive wall, major disruptions occur.}
elements.sent-2411-070: If $q_{75 > 2$, and the wall is resistive, the disruption is minor.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The particular case $q_a = 3$, $q_{75 = 1.75$ is considered in more detail.}
elements.sent-2411-072: A major disruption occurs for a resistive wall.
elements.sent-2411-073: All the other boundary conditions give only minor disruptions.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=2.5cm]{\figdir/fig3a_mst_3685884p6059chop.eps}(a) \includegraphics[height=2.5cm]{\figdir/fig3b_mst_3685883p6390chop.eps}(b) \includegraphics[height=2.5cm]{\figdir/fig3c_mst_3685883ap6390chop.eps}(c) \includegraphics[height=2.5cm]{\figdir/fig3d_mst_3919693p6151chop.eps}(d) \includegraphics[height=2.5cm]{\figdir/fig3e_mst_65640668p4182chop.eps}(e) \end{center} \caption{\it Pressure $p$ contours in nonlinear simulation of the $q_a$ case for (a) ideal wall, (b) resistive wall, (c) perturbed $n \ge 1$ part of magnetic field corresponding to case (b), (d) feedback stabilization, (e) rotating wall. \rfig{lowbeta}(a),(b) reproduced from \cite{nf-iaea24}} \label{fig:lowbeta}

elements.sent-2411-081: The feedback and wall rotation is described in \cite{nf-iaea24 and summarized in the following \rsec{feedback .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Active feedback and wall rotation can make the wall effectively ideal and suppress RWTM major disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-083: There have been extensive theoretical \cite{ bondeson-liu,he,brennan and experimental studies of feedback stabilization \cite{garofalo,okabayashi2009,sabbagh2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: To model feedback, consider the magnetic diffusion equation at a thin resistive wall \cite{jet21,d3d22,finn95 \be \frac{\pd \psi_w {\pd t = \frac{\eta_w {\delta_w ( \psi'_{vac - \psi_p' ) - \Omega_w \frac{\pd \psi {\pd \phi .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The vacuum field is taken of the form \be \psi_{vac = \psi_w \left(\frac{r_w {r}\right)^m + \psi_f \left[ \left(\frac{r_w {r}\right)^m - \left(\frac{r {r_w}\right)^m \right] \ee where $ \psi_f = g D\psi_w/2 - hr_w F\psi_p'/(2m)$ is the feedback signal, $g$ is the normal gain, $h$ is the transverse gain, $D(\theta,\psi_w), F(\theta,\psi_w)$ are screening functions of poloidal and toroidal angle of the wall, modeling the location of the sensors, and $r_w$ is the wall radius.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: For now, take $D = F =1.$ They could be taken non zero in future numerical studies, and might affect detailed predictions of the modeling.
elements.sent-2411-087: The $g$ term models saddle coils which sense $b_n \propto \psi_w$, while $h$ models probes which sense transverse perturbed magnetic field $b_l \propto \psi_p'$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The $\Omega_w$ term models a rotating wall boundary condition \cite{okabayashi2,brennan .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Wall rotation can provide electromagnetic torque to sustain sheared rotation \cite{okabayashi2 .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Then \req{thin0 ,\req{psivac can be expressed \be \frac{\pd \psi_w {\pd t = -\frac{m {\tau_{wall [ (1 - h) \psi_p' + (1+ g) \psi_w / r_w ] - \Omega_w \frac{\pd \psi {\pd \phi .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: More advanced experimental methods vary the feedback gain in time \cite{sabbagh2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-092: The goal here is to demonstrate that feedback or wall rotation can prevent major disruptions, although it can permit minor disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-093: RWM and RWTM can be found together at high $\beta.$ Both can be feedback stabilized.

elements.sent-2411-129: To summarize, resistive wall tearing modes (RWTM) can cause cause major disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-130: For $(m,n) = (2,1)$ modes, the rational surface radius of the $q=2$ surface, normalized to the plasma minor radius, is $\rho_{q2 > 0.75.$ This can also be expressed as the value of $q$ at $\rho = 0.75$, $q_{75 < 2.$ The domain of instability of RWTMs in the $(q_{75 ,\beta)$ plane was presented qualitatively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The $\rho_{q2 > 0.75$ criterion was found in a DIII-D locked mode disruption database.}
elements.sent-2411-132: The importance of mode locking and disruption precursors was discussed.
elements.sent-2411-133: A very important feature of RWTMs is that they produce major disruptions when the $q_{75 < 2$ criterion is satisfied.}
elements.sent-2411-134: If this is not satisfied, or if the wall is ideally conducting, then the mode becomes a tearing mode and does not produce a major disruption, although it can produce a minor disruption.
elements.sent-2411-135: This was verified in simulations of a sequence of low $\beta$ equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-136: It was shown that when the wall is resistive and the $q_{75}$ criterion is satisfied, the saturated mode amplitude is large, otherwise it is small.
elements.sent-2411-137: The computational model used for feedback and rotating wall was discussed.
elements.sent-2411-138: At high $\beta$, feedback stabilized $(2,1)$ modes were observed in NSTX with $\rho_{q2 \approx 0.75,$ indicating wall interaction, implying a RWTM.}
elements.sent-2411-139: In DIII-D, modes with $\rho_{q2 \approx 0.75$ caused a major disruption.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Simulations were performed using modified NSTX equilibria at moderate $\beta_N = 3,$ which satisfied $q_{75 < 2,$ and produced major disruptions with a resistive wall, minor disruptions with an ideal wall, feedback, or rotating wall.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The $q_{75}$ criterion was analyzed in a linear simulations, and a simple geometric model was given.
elements.sent-2411-142: In conclusion, $(m,n) = (2,1)$ RWTMs satisfy the $q_{55 < 2$ condition.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The boundary conditions at the wall can prevent a major disruption.
elements.sent-2411-144: With an ideally conducting wall, tearing modes produce only minor disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-145: Feedback and rotating wall boundary conditions act like an ideal wall.
elements.sent-2411-146: This could potentially eliminate disruptions from tokamaks, greatly enhancing the prospects of magnetic fusion.

elements.sent-2411-148: This work was supported by U.S.. D.O.E.
elements.sent-2411-149: The $q_{75}$ criterion is analyzed in linear simulations, and a simple geometric model is given.
elements.sent-2411-150: After an ELM, a tearing mode developed in shot 131753 with $\rho_{q2 \approx 0.75,$ with growth time $10 ms,$ when the toroidal velocity at $q_{75 \approx 0.$ The mode caused a major disruption, in which the ratio of initial to final $\beta$ dropped from $1$ to less than $0.25.$ It appears that RWTMs were observed in KSTAR \cite{kstar .}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: A simulation based on an equilibrium reconstruction with $\beta_N = 3.7$ found a $(2,1)$ mode with magnetic perturbations extending through a resistive wall, clearly a RWTM although not identified as such.
